ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI exists for Wajik specifically; closest defensible anchor is the glutinous rice food product class. Wajik is a cooked, sweetened glutinous rice confection.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Wajik. A0CKW (rice-based confectionery) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for a sweetened glutinous rice cake.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Wajik or equivalent traditional Southeast Asian glutinous rice confection.

preparation_method: pan-cooking with coconut milk and palm sugar
allergens: tree nuts (coconut — classified as tree nut by some regulatory bodies)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Wajik is one diamond- or square-shaped piece weighing approximately 50 grams, as commonly portioned at Malay and Indonesian festive occasions.

nutrient_source.title: Nutrition in Traditional Sweets and Desserts — Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are based on HPB Singapore data for traditional Malay/Southeast Asian glutinous rice sweets with coconut milk and palm sugar. Wajik is not listed as a standalone entry in HPB FoodData; values are adapted from the closest HPB-listed glutinous rice confection category. Saturated fat estimated from coconut milk contribution (~10g fat, ~9g saturated fat per 100g coconut milk at typical recipe proportions). Monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans fat, cholesterol, and water data not available in the HPB source for this preparation; set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — glutinous rice and palm sugar confections
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Wajik exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 85 is estimated by analogy with cooked glutinous rice (GI ~86–98, University of Sydney GI Database) and the moderating effect of coconut milk fat. Palm sugar has a GI of approximately 35–54 but is present in high concentration. The blended GI of 85 is a defensible high-GI estimate consistent with published glutinous rice GI values. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (85 × 30.25) / 100 = 25.7, rounded to 25.6 for 50g serving (30.25g available carbohydrate = 60.5g × 0.50).

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium content (120 mg/100g) and high caloric density from coconut milk fat and palm sugar
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Wajik to occasional small portions (≤50g) as the moderate sodium and high caloric load can contribute to weight gain, which is an independent risk factor for elevated blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycemic index (~85) driven by glutinous rice and high free sugar content (35g/100g), producing a glycemic load of ~25.6 per 50g serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Wajik is not recommended for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very high GI and high free sugar content; consumption will cause rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation and should be strictly limited or avoided.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat content (~10.8g/100g) from coconut milk and high added sugar (35g/100g) contributing to dyslipidaemia and caloric excess
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption of Wajik, as the high saturated fat from coconut milk and high added sugar content can adversely affect LDL cholesterol and triglyceride levels.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High free sugar content (35g/100g) may promote systemic inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit Wajik intake as high dietary sugar is associated with pro-inflammatory cytokine activity; occasional small portions are preferable to regular consumption.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Wajik (also known as Wajik Ketan in Indonesian or Wajik Pulut in Malay) is a traditional Southeast Asian sweet confection originating from Malaysia and Indonesia, particularly associated with Malay and Javanese culinary traditions. It is made by slow-cooking glutinous rice (pulut) with rich coconut milk and palm sugar (gula melaka) until the mixture becomes intensely sticky, glossy, and deeply caramel-brown in colour. The cooked mass is then pressed into a tray and cut into characteristic diamond or square shapes once cooled and set. Pandan leaves are commonly added during cooking to impart a fragrant, grassy aroma that is a hallmark of Southeast Asian sweets. The texture of Wajik is dense, chewy, and very sticky, with a rich sweetness balanced by the subtle nuttiness of coconut milk and the complex molasses-like depth of palm sugar. Nutritionally, Wajik is energy-dense at approximately 365 kcal per 100g, with a high carbohydrate content (60.5g/100g) dominated by free sugars (35g/100g) from palm sugar and the starchy glutinous rice. The fat content (12.5g/100g) is predominantly saturated fat (~10.8g/100g) derived from coconut milk. Protein content is low at 3.2g/100g. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 85 (high), making Wajik a high-glycemic food that causes rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation. It is not suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular disease. Wajik is a culturally significant food served at Malay and Javanese weddings, Hari Raya celebrations, and other festive occasions. It is vegetarian, vegan, halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free.
common_user_queries: What is the GI of Wajik?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore data for traditional Malay glutinous rice confections; Wajik is not listed as a standalone entry in the HPB FoodData database. Saturated fat is estimated from coconut milk composition at typical recipe proportions. GI is an analogous estimate based on published glutinous rice GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the adapted (not exact) source match.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (10.8g/100g) is estimated from the coconut milk contribution at typical Wajik recipe proportions (~200ml coconut milk per 250g glutinous rice dry weight), consistent with HPB Singapore data for similar coconut milk-based glutinous rice sweets. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation and moisture data for traditionally prepared Wajik are not available in the HPB Singapore database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b12_mcg, and vitamin_d_mcg are reported as 0.0 (confirmed negligible/zero): Wajik contains no animal-derived ingredients contributing these vitamins; values are consistent with HPB data for plant-based glutinous rice confections. 4. GI of 85 is an analogous estimate based on cooked glutinous rice (GI 86–98, University of Sydney GI Database) with a modest downward adjustment for coconut milk fat content; this is a defensible high-GI estimate. Glycemic load per 50g serving = (85 × 30.25) / 100 = 25.6. 5. The input record listed 'low_sodium' as a dietary tag; this has been removed as 120mg sodium/100g does not meet the standard threshold for a low-sodium claim (typically ≤40mg/100g per HPB/FSA guidelines). 6. FoodOn URI and FoodEx2 code are approximate matches; no exact ontology codes exist for Wajik in FoodOn or FoodEx2 as of 2024.
